item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *Happy Filipino Noises*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
she’s lonely in her big house. She said her house was always getting bigger and it would not stop. She told me that sometimes she gets lost in her big house, and it’s full of toys and dresses for her children and her grandchildren, but she is all alone and she can’t get out.

a terrifying depiction of hell

she can contact the world of the living, but all she can think to do with that is try to ruin her daughter's life

but if she could think to do anything else, she wouldn't have become trapped in hell

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I've always seen the Foundation as amoral and willing to do cruelty, of course, but compared to the other organizations, they are by far the nicest, and the most caring. They are the lightest shade of gray in a sea of grays and blacks. I shall defend this belief no matter what.

Stories like this show that the Foundation is indeed capable of good, and that pleases me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm genuinely surprised I haven't upvoted this.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. The author may revert at any time.

+ show block
– hide block

Alright, so, the line "Rest assured that alternate containment procedures were proposed and considered" reminds me of the fact that this article is one of the ones featured in SCP-2305, and that it would be an easy crosslink to make out there. As such, I am suggesting the following:

Editing this:

Rest assured that alternate containment procedures were proposed and considered. The containment procedures that are currently in place were specifically selected and approved by the O5 council for the following reasons:

To this:

Rest assured that alternate containment procedures were proposed and considered. The containment procedures that are currently in place were specifically selected and approved by the O5 council for the following reasons:

item.title: Re: *Nokia tune*
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit, that's it! When I read this piece, rather, the interview, I was thinking "Wow, this sounds literally like my Philippine friends. This sounds exactly how that person would talk!"

I came here to ask if you were from the Philippines yourself or knew someone who was, the answer though was much starker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I nodded after I finished reading. This article is simply good. The buildup of the frustration in the interview log is perfectly resolved in the researcher log.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Broken Engrish made it for me, upvoted with glee, but you may consider placing the interview extract in a collapsible, because it's a massive wall of text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This might be the first story that depicts the foundation as the good guys. We get stories on the reg about how the Foundation is a warmacht like that of America''s but reinforce this idea that "The Foundation sucks but the alternative is far worse". After the first memo I had to re-read the SCP another 4 times because I thought "wouldn't the Foundation just kill Albert so that the supernatural estate lawyers can close the settlement?"

When I saw the trope-inversion in the last memo I was like "this is why I love the writers here".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this one - the mother's expectations felt very real/pressing to me. I fixed what I think was a typo (selection of phone-call recipients based on proximity to where POI 1 was living instead of POI 2). +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a second-gen Fil-Am, can't hammer +1 enough. This sounds a lot like the ghost stories my uncles and cousins would tell at night, and with someone whose parents separated and knows a lot of family who struggled to bring sons and daughters to America to see them succeed (only for them to not follow their parents' definition of success), it hits home.

I'm also loving the subversion of a heartless Foundation, and the odd English syntax in the statement is also true to life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't forget nagging moms, sodas put in bags, and malls bigger than anything back home… :V

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Yeah, lots of people want to leave the Philippines in the hopes of a better life in the US.

They'll be missing out on balut and qwek qwek and mobile postpaid plans that are pretty sweet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Late to the party here, but I found it really refreshing to see the human side of the Foundation. I am happy that they would actively work to keep the family together, because it's the right thing to do. That last note sold it for me.

Upvote +1

item.title: Re: *Nokia tune*
item.content:encoded: This is an episode of Maalaala Mo Kaya waiting to happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this phrase wins it for me… "Anomalous bureaucratic influence"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, this got to me. Well done, Pepper. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is extremely refreshing. It's nice to see a happy ending every now and then. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi Peppers! ;>
The small precision about the mother's death makes everything much clearer - BUT you overlooked a blooper:

was found to have been deceased for several years

That's soon after the kidnapping in 2008, and she died in 2007, so it's not several years. 'deceased for some time' would work.

Yes, I agree with you that there is no need to say more about the mother's ghost's nature.

Another mistake (I overlooked it the first time, I'm afraid…):
(at the beginning of the daughter's ITW)

I told her “No, I have already have a boyfriend here.”

You have twice 'have'. It could be deliberate, but I don't think so.

Aaaaaaaand… that's all. I do believe the simple addition of the mother's death date clarified the rest! I'm off to upvote now… ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure! Peppers works fine. Thanks a ton for the critique! I've made some adjustments based on your feedback.

+ A few responses
– hide block

- I've added a line elaborating slightly on the mother's death. The SCP is the mother's ghost, trapped in a supernatural perpetuation of her behavior during life. However, the Foundation can't scientifically prove it's the mother's "soul", so they only wrote that the entity shares her personality and voice.
- I'll leave the nature of the mother's ghost vague in the article itself. It's one of those things that I think, in this case, works better when it's loosely implied than explicitly stated.
- Just because she was held at a Foundation site doesn't mean they told her anything about anomalies. Foundation information is on a need-to-know basis, and she didn't need to know anything more than she already did. Given her background, it's only natural for her to assume that the phone calls from her dead mother are supernatural in nature.

Does this also apply when a 500-dollar wire transfer is made to SCP-2190's latest target? Not clear…

Yep. Fridge logic. Again, I feel this is a case where the information is implied strongly enough that there's not much necessity in stating it explicitly.

- I changed the line to "Suspected anomalous bureaucratic influence is being investigated."
- Much of the daughter's story is true to real events. I'm sorry it was too long for you. I considered trimming the story before I posted this, but I decided that the best way to respect the original events would be to describe them as completely as possible.

Don't worry though—I'm not offended at all! I really appreciate constructive feedback. This may be based on a true story, but it should be subjected to the same level of criticism as any other piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oooooooooh. Not good (though in a good way): this reminds me of something… or rather, someone… best left alone where she is now…

Peppers (may I call you Peppers? :-) ): this could be excellent for all the reasons mentioned above and a few more, but… there is a big but, explained below:

+ show block
– hide block

- when did the mother (the actual human person, not the SCP) die? Did she die in the US? When did the misery begin: before the human mother died, or after? Is the SCP a mere continuation of the mother's obsession with her daughter? Not clear, IMO…
- if the daughter knows her mother is dead, why does she still think that her mother has been/is responsible for her misery? She has been living at a Foundation site for a while; she knows about anomalies. Why doesn't she 'detach' herself from her guilt about her mother, by putting the blame on an anomaly? Is it because the Foundation administered amnestics when she moved out? And if this is the case, perhaps Dr Bostenero should mention somewhere in his letter that he regrets having to do this, but there was no choice…

Any attempt to close, transfer ownership, or edit the balance of POI-2190-1's bank account via computerized systems will result in a system error, and any alterations to physical documents pertaining to SCP-2190's wealth will anomalously revert the following day.

Does this also apply when a 500-dollar wire transfer is made to SCP-2190's latest target? Not clear…

Anomalous bureaucratic influence is suspected.

Can't the Foundation investigate into this? If not 'humanly' (because bank clerks keep forgetting), at least electronically - I mean, like tapping the phones of the bank-clerks, reading their emails, stuff like that…

Finally, a personal opinion: I think the daughter's statement is too long, too full of details and events.
But I want to add, concerning this latest opinion as well as the rest of my comments: I apologize in advance if this skip actually describes all or part of something that happened to you, or someone close to you, and if I consequently offended you with this post.

All in all, a good potential skip… if only because it makes you think and read carefully.
No vote for now. I don't want to downvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Peppers, your articles always tug at my feel strings. In this case, I have to echo Accelerando's mention of the "bigger house" sentence, it's simply perfect… And the somewhat broken English of the letter adds to the whole. It's a beautiful piece… not that you need me to say it at this point. 🐶 Congratulations. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The human or humanizing element here is something I can wholly support. Every so often one forgets why the Foundation does what it does, and this is a good example of the reason — to subtract, a little at a time, from the world's surfeit of uncertainty, misery, and fear. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An excellent and humanizing article that shows the Foundation can be something other than a huge, unfeeling monster. Good feels are good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic! I think the interview is a very solid piece of gothic human tragedy/magical realism, except that you've bookended it with just what the Foundation thinks of "magical realism" and how they deal with it. It's a bit of a genre sandwich, but I'm so glad you pulled it off, and that it's on the wiki.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I cannot love what she does to my family, but she is still my mother, and I hate the things she does but I still love.

This and the line about the house getting bigger got to me, and I didn't expect it to. Being told about that Christian mindset in regards to loving your family and enemies, it's even more chilling with a mother that just won't leave her daughter alone.

Amazing as always, Peppers. +1/+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad. +1

I fixed two things for ya, check the edit history for details and correct if necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had my doubts at first, but I thoroughly enjoyed the read. +1

item.title: *Nokia tune*
item.content:encoded:
I wrote this several months ago and it's been festering in my sandbox all this time, so I figured I may as well take the plunge and post it.

This is very different from the sort of stories I usually tell, and it was very difficult for me to write, because about 95% of it actually happened. It's not often that an SCP can be said to be based on a true story, but this one certainly is.

This story haunted me for a long, long time. Imagine a woman claiming to be your mother suddenly appearing out of the blue and doing everything she can to control your life. Imagine constantly living in fear of strangers attacking you or your family because they were bribed by an obsessed, lonely woman. It's a uniquely terrifying concept for me. Thankfully, those involved have found peace.

Discover more of my weird ramblings in
~PeppersGhost's Gallery of Illusion!~
